") [Versions Notes](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916/notes) Abstract -------- With the continuous improvement of global technological levels and the increasing demand for high-performance alloy materials in national economic construction, the traditional single principal component alloy is increasingly unable to meet people’s increasing service needs. High-entropy alloys play an important role in aerospace, mechanical manufacturing, biomedicine, energy development and other engineering fields because of their unique physical, chemical and mechanical properties. Based on the concept of high-entropy alloys, the high-entropy effect, lattice distortion effect, sluggish diffusion effect and cocktail effect of high-entropy alloys are represented in this paper. The common preparation methods of high-entropy alloys are summarized according to the classification of melting-casting method, mechanical alloying method and coating method. The strength and toughness, wear resistance, corrosion resistance, high temperature resistance, fatigue resistance, radiation resistance and magnetic properties of the high-entropy alloys are discussed. The application prospect of high-entropy alloys is summarized, and the future research and development direction of high-entropy alloys are prospected on this basis. Keywords: [high-entropy alloys](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=high-entropy+alloys); [preparation methods](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=preparation+methods); [wear resistance](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=wear+resistance); [corrosion resistance](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=corrosion+resistance); [application prospect](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=application+prospect) 1. Introduction --------------- As one of the three pillars of modern civilization, materials play an irreplaceable role in the process of continuous development of society. In the course of the advancement and evolution of human history from the Stone Age to the Bronze Age and then to the Iron Age, every major social change was accompanied by the emergence of new materials. The first technological revolution is based on the development of materials such as steel and copper. The second technological revolution is based on the development of alloy steel, aluminum alloy and various non-metallic materials. The third technological revolution has realized large-scale industrialization and civil production of synthetic materials and semiconductor materials. Materials are the basis of modern technology and the core of all scientific and technological progress. The development of materials has not only changed the way of human life, but has also promoted the progress of productivity and the innovation of science and technology. Composite materials, amorphous materials and high-entropy alloys are the three hotspots in the field of materials in the past 20 years. In a socio-economically developed environment with the theme of informatization, intelligent accelerated development, energy saving and emission reduction, improving the comprehensive performance of alloys is the overall goal of new material research and development. As a new type of alloy with high hardness, high heat resistance, strong corrosion resistance, high wear resistance and fatigue resistance, high-entropy alloys are different from any existing traditional alloy. As a new alloy system, high-entropy alloys have important research value and broad application prospects. Therefore, the design, preparation and application of high-entropy alloys have become an important direction for the development of new materials [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#B1-coatings-13-01916),[2](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#B2-coatings-13-01916)]. 2. Concept of High-Entropy Alloys --------------------------------- #### 2.1. Proposal of High-Entropy Alloys As early as the end of the 18th century, Franz Karl Achard carried out experimental research on multicomponent alloys of five to seven elements. The innovative work was not discovered and reported until 1963 by Professor Cyril Stanley Smith, which is the earliest recorded study on high-entropy alloys. In 1993, Professor Cantor of Cambridge University first expounded the concept of multiprincipal alloys [[3](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#B3-coatings-13-01916)]. In the subsequent research work, Cantor [[4](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#B4-coatings-13-01916)] also pointed out that the traditional alloy design concept caused the lack of research on the alloy phase diagram. Under the traditional alloy design concept, the research on the endpoint or edge region of the phase diagram has been greatly developed, while the large area in the middle of the phase diagram is poorly understood, especially the phase diagram analysis of the alloy in the presence of equal moles of several or more elements. As shown in [Figure 1](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#fig_body_display_coatings-13-01916-f001), there is a large degree of blank area in the phase diagram of binary and ternary alloys. Therefore, it is necessary to expand and supplement the alloy phase diagram. It is generally believed that there are two ways to achieve this goal. One is to increase the alloy element content on the basis of traditional alloy design, and the other is to use an equal atom substitution method, that is, to replace individual atoms in the component with other elements. Through the above two methods, a multicomponent alloy system with closer composition can be obtained. ![Image 7: Coatings 13 01916 g001](https://www.mdpi.com/coatings/coatings-13-01916/article_deploy/html/images/coatings-13-01916-g001-550.jpg)[](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#fig_body_display_coatings-13-01916-f001) **Figure 1.** Schematic diagram of ternary and quaternary alloy systems [[5](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#B5-coatings-13-01916)]. Around 1995, scholars represented by Professor Yeh have also carried out a lot of basic research on multiprincipal alloy [[6](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#B6-coatings-13-01916),[7](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/13/11/1916#B7-coatings-13-01916)]. all metrics](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6/metrics) Abstract -------- High-entropy alloys (HEAs) have emerged as promising materials with exceptional mechanical properties, thus, making them suitable for demanding applications that require high strength and toughness. This review highlights the latest advancements in HEA research, focusing on processing techniques, microstructural evolution, and mechanical properties and behavior techniques. The unique HEA composition comprising four or more principal elements in almost equiatomic ratios produces high configurational entropy, which stabilizes distinct solid solution phases. This stabilization leads to microstructures distinguished by disorder and set of defect types, generating superior mechanical properties. For several years, substantial advancement has been achieved in HEA processing routes, from traditional casting and powder metallurgy to state-of-the-art methods such as additive manufacturing and severe plastic deformation. These processes, combined with a developing understanding of microstructural evolution and phase transformations, are broadening the potential application of HEAs in aerospace, energy, and automotive. As the field progresses, the implications of these findings are shaping future research directions and expanding the potential applications of HEAs in various industries. This review underscores the current challenges in tailoring the microstructure of HEAs and optimizing their mechanical functionality, laying the foundation for future innovations and practical use. manuscript. 1 Introduction -------------- The advent of high-entropy alloys (HEAs) signifies a pivotal evolution in metallurgical design, moving away from conventional alloying strategies that depend predominantly on a singular base element supplemented by minimal solute additions. In contrast, HEAs are constructed from a multitude of principal elements—typically five or more—present in equiatomic or nearly equiatomic ratios, resulting in a substantial configurational entropy that thermodynamically promotes the development of disordered solid solutions rather than intermetallic compounds [[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR1 "Miracle DB, Senkov ON (2017) A critical review of high entropy alloys and related concepts. Acta Mater 122:448–511"), [2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR2 "Murty BS et al (2019) High-entropy alloys, Elsevier")]. This compositional intricacy, combined with distinctive phase stabilization mechanisms, has positioned HEAs at the vanguard of advanced materials research for applications in extreme environments. Initiated by Yeh and associates in the early 2000s, the high-entropy paradigm introduced an innovative pathway to attain mechanical and thermal resilience through the entropic stabilization of phases [[3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR3 "Yeh JW et al (2004) Nanostructured high-entropy alloys with multiple principal elements: novel alloy design concepts and outcomes. Adv Eng Mater 6(5):299–303")]. The resultant alloys often display straightforward crystallographic configurations such as face-centered cubic (FCC), body-centered cubic (BCC), or a dual-phase amalgamation, notwithstanding their multicomponent nature [[4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR4 "Cantor B et al (2004) Microstructural development in equiatomic multicomponent alloys. Mater Sci Eng A 375:213–218")]. This apparent simplicity can be attributed to sluggish diffusion, lattice distortion, and cocktail effects, which collectively inhibit phase segregation and intermetallic formation—characteristics that confer exceptional combinations of strength, ductility, wear resistance, and thermal stability [[5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR5 "Zhang Y et al (2014) Microstructures and properties of high-entropy alloys. Prog Mater Sci 61:1–93"), [6](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR6 "Gludovatz B, Ritchie RO (2022) Fracture properties of high-entropy alloys. MRS Bull 47(2):176–185")]. Contemporary research endeavors concerning HEA design have underscored the significance of compositional optimization and phase stability modeling employing thermodynamic frameworks such as CALPHAD and first-principles calculations. These methodologies enable predictive governance over solid solution formation, phase transitions, and microstructural evolution under diverse thermomechanical conditions [[7](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR7 "Zhang Y, Li R (2020) New advances in high-entropy alloys. Entropy 22:1158"), [8](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR8 "Ren J-T et al (2023) High-entropy alloys in electrocatalysis: from fundamentals to applications. Chem Soc Rev 52(23):8319–8373")]. In parallel, experimental initiatives have concentrated on the refinement of processing methodologies—spanning vacuum arc melting and powder metallurgy to more advanced techniques such as additive manufacturing (AM) and severe plastic deformation (SPD). Such methods facilitate precise regulation of grain size, defect density, and phase morphology, thereby customizing performance to meet specific industrial requisites [[9](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR9 "Shiratori H et al (2016) Relationship between the microstructure and mechanical properties of an equiatomic AlCoCrFeNi high-entropy alloy fabricated by selective electron beam melting. Mater Sci Eng A 656:39–46"),[10](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR10 "Park JM et al (2020) Superior tensile properties of 1% C-CoCrFeMnNi high-entropy alloy additively manufactured by selective laser melting. Mater Res Lett 8(1):1–7"),[11](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR11 "Li J et al (2019) Additive manufacturing of high-strength CrMnFeCoNi high-entropy alloys-based composites with WC addition. J Mater Sci Technol 35(11):2430–2434")]. Additive manufacturing, in particular, has facilitated unprecedented versatility in the design and production of HEA components. By enabling rapid solidification, localized compositional regulation, and geometrical intricacy, AM not only improves microstructural uniformity but also accelerates the advancement of application-specific HEA architectures [[12](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR12 "Kenel C, Casati NPM, Dunand DC (2019) 3D ink-extrusion additive manufacturing of CoCrFeNi high-entropy alloy micro-lattices. Nat Commun 10(1):904")]. Similarly, SPD methodologies such as high-pressure torsion (HPT) and equal-channel angular pressing (ECAP) have demonstrated their efficacy in achieving nanocrystalline structures that enhance strength through grain boundary hardening [[13](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR13 "Shi P et al (2019) Enhanced strength–ductility synergy in ultrafine-grained eutectic high-entropy alloys by inheriting microstructural lamellae. Nat Commun 10(1):489")]. Notwithstanding significant advancements, a few critical challenges remain. The mechanisms dictating phase formation in multi-principal-element systems are not yet fully elucidated, particularly under non-equilibrium processing conditions. Additionally, the correlations between atomic-scale disorder, defect structures, and macroscopic mechanical behavior continue to be subjects of active inquiry. Hence, the adjustment of the trade-off related to strength and ductility, along with the advancement of fracture toughness and fatigue resistance, necessitates a deeper fusion of experimental characterization and multiscale modeling [[14](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR14 "Feng J et al (2021) Microstructures and mechanical properties of reduced activation ferritic/martensitic steel fabricated by laser melting deposition. Fusion Eng Des 173:112865"),[15](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR15 "Hassan MA et al (2021) Improvement ductility and corrosion resistance of CoCrFeNi and AlCoCrFeNi HEAs by electroless copper technique. J Mater Res Technol 13:463–485"),[16](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR16 "Liang C et al (2022) Mechanical and tribological properties of (FeCoNi)88–x(AlTi)12Mox high-entropy alloys. Int J Refract Metal Hard Mater 105:105845")]. This review endeavors to provide a thorough assessment of the current advancements in HEA research, concentrating on the interrelations among fabrication techniques, microstructural transformations, and mechanical responses. Its objective is to delineate key strategies for surmounting performance trade-offs and to guide the rational design of next-generation HEAs tailored for high-performance structural and functional applications. 2 Fabrication Techniques for High-Entropy Alloys (HEAs) ------------------------------------------------------- ### 2.1 Traditional Casting and Alloying Methods The application of traditional casting and alloying methodologies has been profoundly employed in the synthesis of HEAs, offering a proficient approach for the production of substantial quantities of materials characterized by controlled compositions and microstructures. Conventional casting techniques, including arc melting, induction melting, and vacuum melting, are routinely employed to fabricate HEAs from either elemental constituents or pre-alloyed powders (Fig.[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#Fig1)). These methodologies involve the melting and amalgamation of the discrete components within a containment vessel, generally a crucible, while ensuring a regulated atmosphere free from reactive agents or atmospheric gases. The resultant liquid alloy is subsequently cast into molds to form ingots or sizable samples. Arc melting is favored for its ability to accommodate a broad spectrum of alloy compositions and to produce homogenous melts that are suitable for subsequent processing [[17](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR17 "Saha M (2024) 3 Additive manufacturing of compositionally complex alloys: trends, challenges, and future perspectives. 3D Printing Technologies: Digital Manufacturing, Artificial Intelligence, Industry 4.0, p 61")]. These methodologies are integral to the mechanical characteristics and microstructural evolution HEAs. Established techniques such as arc melting, induction melting, and vacuum melting are routinely employed to synthesize HEAs, facilitating the uniform amalgamation of numerous principal constituents, which is imperative for attaining a stable single-phase solid solution or multiphase configurations [[5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR5 "Zhang Y et al (2014) Microstructures and properties of high-entropy alloys. Prog Mater Sci 61:1–93")]. Nonetheless, these methodologies may engender microsegregation, residual stresses, and variations in grain size, which can adversely influence the overall tensile strength, ductility, and corrosion resistance of HEAs [[8](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR8 "Ren J-T et al (2023) High-entropy alloys in electrocatalysis: from fundamentals to applications. Chem Soc Rev 52(23):8319–8373")]. Moreover, casting parameters including cooling rates and mold materials significantly affect the ultimate phase development and defect density, thereby influencing the mechanical performance and thermal stability of HEAs [[4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR4 "Cantor B et al (2004) Microstructural development in equiatomic multicomponent alloys. Mater Sci Eng A 375:213–218")]. Although traditional methods establish a foundational basis for HEA advancement, innovative processing techniques such as additive manufacturing and powder metallurgy are currently under investigation to enhance microstructural regulation and consistency of properties. **Fig.1** [![Image 6: figure 1](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs40735-025-00999-6/MediaObjects/40735_2025_999_Fig1_HTML.png)](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6/figures/1) **a** The laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) framework, (direct metal laser sintering methodology innovated by EOS GmbH), and the associated lexicon pertaining to LPBF, **b** electron beam melting machine (EBM), **c** selective laser melting (SLM) machine, (primary components). Reprinted with permission from [[18](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR18 "Khan HM et al (2020) Influence of the post-processing operations on surface integrity of metal components produced by laser powder bed fusion additive manufacturing: a review. Mach Sci Technol 25(1):118–176"), [19](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR19 "Teixeira Ó et al (2020) A review of heat treatments on improving the quality and residual stresses of the Ti–6Al–4V parts produced by additive manufacturing. Metals 10(8):1006")] [Full size image](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6/figures/1) ### 2.2 Powder Metallurgy and Mechanical Alloying Mechanical alloying (MA) alongside powder metallurgy (PM) serves as adaptable production techniques for HEAs, enabling exact supervision of their compositional details, microstructural qualities, and mechanical functionalities. MA constitutes a technique for processing powders in a solid state. As illustrated in Fig.[2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#Fig2), this approach involves the utilization of a high-energy ball mill to mechanically grind elemental powders. This procedure culminates in the formation of finely dispersed alloy powders that exhibit enhanced reactivity and uniformity in composition. It permits the fabrication of HEAs possessing complex compositions that are difficult to achieve through conventional casting methods. As a high-energy ball milling process, it promotes intense plastic deformation and mixing at the atomic level, facilitating the formation of a homogenous solid solution or nanocrystalline. Meanwhile, PM denotes a method that consolidates elemental or pre-alloyed powders through approaches like cold pressing, sintering, and hot isostatic pressing (HIP). This leads to the fabrication of dense components exhibiting specific microstructures that closely approximate the intended final geometry. The versatility of PM facilitates the incorporation of diverse alloying elements and additives, thereby enabling the formulation of intricate HEA compositions characterized by enhanced uniformity and microstructures comprising fine grains. Furthermore, the employment of PM techniques permits the production of HEA components with meticulous regulation of their porosity and grain size. This engenders opportunities for the customization of mechanical properties such as improved hardness, wear resistance, and thermal stability, to fit specific application needs [[10](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR10 "Park JM et al (2020) Superior tensile properties of 1% C-CoCrFeMnNi high-entropy alloy additively manufactured by selective laser melting. Mater Res Lett 8(1):1–7")]. **Fig.2** [![Image 7: figure 2](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs40735-025-00999-6/MediaObjects/40735_2025_999_Fig2_HTML.png)](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6/figures/2) Conventional fabrication techniques **a** the Arc-melting method, **b** vacuum induction method. Reprinted with permission from [[20](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR20 "Callister WD Jr, Rethwisch DG (2020) Materials science and engineering: an introduction. Wiley, Hoboken"), [21](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR21 "Askeland DR et al (2003) The science and engineering of materials. 357–374")] [Full size image](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6/figures/2) The consolidation of mechanically alloyed powders through methods like hot pressing or spark plasma sintering (SPS) enables the creation of bulk HEAs materials with customized mechanical characteristics and improved performance [[24](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR24 "Sistla HR, Newkirk JW, Liou FF (2015) Effect of Al/Ni ratio, heat treatment on phase transformations and microstructure of AlxFeCoCrNi2−x (x= 0.3, 1) high entropy alloys. Mater Des 81:113–121")], the schematic operational procedure for SPS is given in Fig.[3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#Fig3). Furthermore, the integration of PM and MA processes provides synergistic benefits in the production of HEAs, enabling the creation of new materials with exceptional properties. One way to create highly reactive pre-alloyed powders is using mechanical alloying. These powders can then be compressed using PM techniques to produce fully dense components made of HEAs with regulated microstructures. Incorporating alloying elements and reinforcements during the mechanical alloying process allows for the creation of functionally graded high-entropy composites. These composites can be customized to have specific properties for various applications, such as wear-resistant coatings and high-temperature structural materials [[25](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR25 "Lilensten L et al (2018) Study of a bcc multi-principal element alloy: tensile and simple shear properties and underlying deformation mechanisms. Acta Mater 142:131–141")]. However, PM is limited by residual porosity, high powder costs, and the need for additional densification processes like HIP to achieve full density and optimal properties. **Fig.3** [![Image 8: figure 3](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs40735-025-00999-6/MediaObjects/40735_2025_999_Fig3_HTML.png)](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6/figures/3) Procedure for amorphization by mechanical alloying. Reprinted with permission from [[22](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR22 "Urban P et al (2019) Amorphous Al-Ti powders prepared by mechanical alloying and consolidated by electrical resistance sintering. Metals 9(11):1140")] [Full size image](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6/figures/3) ### 2.3 Additive Manufacturing Additive manufacturing (AM), commonly referred to as 3D printing, has become a highly promising method for creating HEAs. This approach provides exceptional versatility in design, allowing for rapid prototyping and the manufacture of intricate shapes with customized microstructures and characteristics. AM techniques, such as laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) (Fig.[4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#Fig4)a), which include selective laser melting (SLM) (Fig.[4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#Fig4)c), and electron beam melting (EBM) (Fig.[4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#Fig4)b), facilitate the layer-by-layer fabrication of metal components from powdered feedstock [[18](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR18 "Khan HM et al (2020) Influence of the post-processing operations on surface integrity of metal components produced by laser powder bed fusion additive manufacturing: a review. Mach Sci Technol 25(1):118–176"), [19](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR19 "Teixeira Ó et al (2020) A review of heat treatments on improving the quality and residual stresses of the Ti–6Al–4V parts produced by additive manufacturing. Metals 10(8):1006")]. This process enables the creation of near-net-shape components with exceptional accuracy and resolution. HEAs produced via AM display distinct microstructural characteristics, such as refined grain patterns and homogeneous dispersion of alloying components. As a result, these materials demonstrate improved mechanical qualities and performance when compared to conventionally manufactured materials [[26](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR26 "Li W et al (2021) Mechanical behavior of high-entropy alloys. Prog Mater Sci 118:100777")]. Additionally, the rapid cooling rates in AM promote the formation of metastable phases and solid solutions, contributing to enhanced mechanical properties such as high tensile strength and fracture toughness [[11](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR11 "Li J et al (2019) Additive manufacturing of high-strength CrMnFeCoNi high-entropy alloys-based composites with WC addition. J Mater Sci Technol 35(11):2430–2434")]. **Fig.4** [![Image 9: figure 4](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs40735-025-00999-6/MediaObjects/40735_2025_999_Fig4_HTML.png)](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6/figures/4) Image of the working principles of SPS. Int J Lightweight Mater Manuf 7(1):14–24")] [Full size image](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6/figures/4) SLM and EBM are frequently utilized additive manufacturing techniques for the fabrication of HEAs, offering advantages such as accelerated processing times, reduced material wastage, and the ability to produce complex geometries. SLM, a methodology that utilizes a high-energy laser to selectively melt and consolidate metal powders incrementally in accordance with computer-aided design (CAD) specifications. This technique facilitates the production of fully dense HEA components characterized by tailored microstructures and mechanical properties. Conversely, EBM, employs an electron beam to selectively melt metal powders within a controlled high-vacuum environment. This process enables the fabrication of HEAs with meticulous regulation of porosity and microstructural gradients. The implementation of these additive manufacturing methodologies engenders opportunities for the development of HEA components endowed with superior mechanical attributes and performance, thus making them suitable for a wide spectrum of applications in the aerospace, automotive, and biomedical sectors [[27](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40735-025-00999-6#ref-CR27 "Li B et al (2020) Selective laser melting of CoCrFeNiMn high entropy alloy powder modified with nano-TiN particles for additive manufacturing and strength enhancement: process, particle behavior and effects. Powder Technol 360:509–521")]. Moreover, the adaptability of AM permits the incorporation of various materials and reinforcements when creating an object, which in turn allows for the creation of functionally graded HEAs composites with customized properties that are suitable for certain uses. Researchers can boost the strength, wear resistance, and thermal stability of HEAs composites by strategically adding alloying elements, ceramic particles, or reinforcement fibers to the feedstock used in AM. This allows for the development of advanced materials through improved design and production techniques. Abstract -------- High-entropy alloys (HEAs) have emerged as an innovative family of multi-principal element alloys with unique properties that position them as potential candidates for the fourth industrial revolution (Industry 4.0) materials. Characterized by their multi-principal element composition, HEAs exhibit exceptional mechanical strength, thermal stability, corrosion resistance, and tailored functional properties. They leverage high configurational entropy to deliver superior performance over traditional alloys such as steel, aluminum, and titanium. HEAs have demonstrated remarkable potential in critical sectors, including aerospace, automotive, energy, and biomedicine, with examples like NbMoTaW in jet engines and TiZrNbTaMo in medical implants showcasing their versatility under extreme conditions. However, challenges such as high processing costs, difficulties in large-scale production, limited understanding of phase stability, and the need for advanced computational models to predict material behavior must be addressed. This paper explores the prospects of HEAs as the fourth industrial material, discussing their advantages, potential applications, and the limitations that must be overcome to realize their full industrial potential. By integrating emerging manufacturing techniques such as additive manufacturing and computational material design, HEAs could revolutionize material engineering and contribute significantly to Industry 4.0. Transitioning from niche innovations to industrial mainstays, mirroring the defining impact of steel in the First Industrial Revolution and silicon in the Third, thereby cementing their place at the forefront of Industry 4.0’s high-performance and sustainable future. * [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000958) * Next article in issue Keywords -------- High entropy alloy 4 th Industrial revolution Thermal and mechanical properties Sustainability 1. Introduction --------------- ### 1.1. Background on industrial materials Industrial materials have evolved remarkably, shaping the trajectory of human innovation and technological progress. In their earliest forms, traditional materials like wood, stone, and basic metals such as copper and iron defined the limits of engineering and construction [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib1),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib2)]. The Bronze Age marked a significant leap with the discovery of alloying, combining metals like copper and tin to create more substantial and durable materials [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib3)]. This was followed by the Iron Age, where iron and steel became cornerstones of industrial development due to their abundance and versatility. These foundational materials powered the first waves of industrialization, from rudimentary tools to the machinery of the First and Second Industrial Revolutions [[[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib4), [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib5), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib6)]. As industries grew more complex, so did the demand for materials with enhanced properties. The 20th century ushered in modern alloy combinations like stainless steel, titanium alloys, and aluminum blends tailored for specific applications in aerospace, automotive, and manufacturing sectors [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib7),[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib8)]. These alloys improved strength, corrosion resistance, and lightweight performance, aligning with the Third Industrial Revolution’s focus on precision, automation, and electronics [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib3)]. Alongside metals, synthetic polymers and ceramics emerged, broadening the material palette for specialized uses [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib9)]. Today, the Fourth Industrial Revolution, characterized by smart manufacturing, nanotechnology, and sustainability, drives the development of advanced materials [[[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib4), [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib5), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib6)]. High-entropy alloys (HEAs) stand out as a promising candidate in this era. Unlike traditional alloys dominated by one principal element, HEAs blend multiple elements in near-equal proportions, yielding unique properties like exceptional strength, thermal stability, and resistance to wear [[[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib10), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib11), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib12)]. First conceptualized in the early 2000s, HEAs represent a paradigm shift, leveraging entropy to stabilize complex structures that defy conventional metallurgy [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib13)]. They hold potential for cutting-edge applications, such as turbine blades, biomedical implants, and energy storage systems, aligning with the demands of Industry 4.0 for multifunctional, high-performance materials [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib14),[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib15)]. The Ashby map shown in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#fig1) offers an insightful overview of material development spanning 10,000 years [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib16)]. It clearly illustrates the different categories of materials, such as ceramics, metals, polymers, and, more recently, composites. The map effectively showcases the progression from discovery to development and, ultimately, to the design of these materials. ![Image 11: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2589234725000922-gr1.jpg) 1. Historical evolution of engineering materials marked with the birth of HEAs published in Advanced Engineering Materials [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib16)]. Despite the advanced properties exhibited by HEAs, traditional alloys, including steels, titanium alloys, and aluminum-based materials, frequently demonstrate superior suitability for specific applications. This preference can be attributed to their cost-effectiveness, established processing methodologies, and proven long-term reliability. In cost-sensitive industries such as automotive manufacturing and construction, mild and low-alloy steels are particularly favored due to their low cost, ease of welding, and scalable production capabilities. Conversely, HEAs, which often incorporate costly elements such as Co, Ni, or Mo, and necessitate complex processing techniques such as vacuum arc melting or powder metallurgy, are generally considered economically unfeasible for large-scale applications [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib17)]. In the realm of biomedical applications, titanium alloys, notably Ti–6Al–4V, continue to dominate due to their established biocompatibility, corrosion resistance, and extensive clinical utility attributes that many HEAs have not yet consistently demonstrated or successfully navigated through regulatory scrutiny [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib18)]. Similarly, in cryogenic environments, such as liquefied natural gas storage and aerospace applications, traditional austenitic stainless steels (e.g., 304L and 316L) outshine many HEAs by maintaining exceptional ductility and fracture toughness at low temperatures. In contrast, HEAs tend to encounter challenges related to phase instability or embrittlement under such conditions [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib19)]. Regarding electrical and thermal conductivity, copper and aluminum alloys remain indispensable in electronics, motors, and heat exchangers. HEAs typically exhibit significantly lower conductivity, a consequence of severe lattice distortion and complex scattering phenomena [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib20)]. Furthermore, in high-performance aerospace engines, conventional nickel-based superalloys, such as Inconel 718, are still preferentially utilized due to their validated high-temperature creep resistance, oxidation protection, and established coating systems, attributes that HEAs have yet to replicate comprehensively under prolonged operational stress [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib21)]. Lastly, in manufacturing environments where formability, weldability, and machinability are paramount, traditional alloys benefit from decades of industrial optimization, robust supply chains, and predictable mechanical behavior. In contrast, many HEAs face obstacles related to high hardness, limited forming data, and a lack of standardized processing protocols [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib22)]. These considerations underscore that, while HEAs offer innovative property combinations in laboratory-scale investigations, traditional alloys remain essential in a wide array of engineering applications due to their cost efficiency, mature processing techniques, and verified long-term performance. ### 1.2. High entropy alloys High entropy alloys represent an emergent class of materials that have captured the attention of scientists and engineers in recent decades. Emerging in the early 2000s, HEAs diverge from the traditional alloy design, which typically relies on one primary element (like Fe in steel or Al in its alloys) mixed with small amounts of others [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib10),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib12),[[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib23), [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib24), [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib25)]. Instead, HEAs are defined as alloys composed of five or more principal elements, each present in near-equal atomic proportions, typically between 5% and 35% [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib26),[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib27)]. This multi-element approach maximizes configurational entropy, a thermodynamic property stabilizing their structure, giving rise to a single-phase solid solution rather than a brittle mix of compounds [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib12),[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib25),[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib28)]. First introduced by researchers like Jien-Wei Yeh and Brian Cantor, HEAs challenge conventional metallurgy and offer a new frontier for material innovation [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib22),[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib29)]. The unique characteristics of HEAs stem from this high-entropy design. One standout feature is their exceptional mechanical strength, often retained across a wide range of temperatures [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib30)]. This arises from lattice distortions caused by the variety of atomic sizes, which impede dislocation movement, a key factor in deformation [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib31),[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib32)]. HEAs also exhibit impressive corrosion resistance, as their complex chemistry can form protective oxide layers or resist degradation in harsh environments [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib33),[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib34)]. Thermal stability is another hallmark; many HEAs maintain their structure and properties at elevated temperatures where traditional alloys might soften or fail [[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib35)]. Additional traits, like wear resistance and ductility, vary depending on composition, making HEAs highly tunable for specific needs [[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib36),[37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib37)]. In the context of "The Prospect and Limitation of High Entropy Alloy as 4th Industrial Material," HEAs hold immense promise for the Fourth Industrial Revolution’s demands. Their multifunctionality suits advanced applications, such as jet engine components enduring extreme heat, biomedical implants resisting bodily corrosion, or lightweight, durable parts for smart manufacturing systems [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib12),[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib19),[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib38)]. The ability to tailor HEAs by adjusting their elemental mix aligns with Industry 4.0’s emphasis on customization, sustainability, and performance under complex conditions. For instance, alloys like CoCrFeNiMn have shown remarkable toughness at cryogenic temperatures, hinting at uses in space exploration or energy storage [[[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib39), [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib40), [[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib41), [[42]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib42)]. However, HEAs face significant limitations. Their complex compositions can complicate manufacturing processes, increase costs, and pose challenges in scalability compared to established alloys like steel or titanium. Additionally, while their properties are impressive in lab settings, real-world performance under diverse conditions remains under scrutiny. The prospect of HEAs as a "4th Industrial Material" hinges on overcoming these hurdles, balancing innovation with practicality to integrate them into the smart, interconnected systems of the future. From humble stone tools to the promise of entropy-driven alloys, the journey of industrial materials reflects humanity’s relentless pursuit of progress, each step building on the last yet always reaching for what lies beyond. ### 1.3. Importance of HEAs in industry High-entropy alloys are poised to play a pivotal role in modern industry, particularly as a potential "4th Industrial Material" aligning with the Fourth Industrial Revolution’s emphasis on smart, sustainable, and high-performance systems. Their superior properties position them as a versatile solution to meet the demands of cutting-edge technologies, from aerospace to biomedical engineering [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib30),[43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib43),[44](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib44)]. As industries evolve toward automation, nanotechnology, and interconnected systems, HEAs offer a compelling case for driving innovation where traditional materials like steel, aluminum, or titanium alloys may fall short. One of the key reasons HEAs are considered a game-changer is their mechanical prowess. Their high strength, often maintained across extreme temperatures, makes them ideal for applications under harsh conditions [[[45]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib45), [[46]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib46), [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib47)]. For instance, in aerospace, where components like turbine blades must endure intense heat and stress, HEAs such as those based on refractory elements (e.g., NbMoTaW) outperform conventional superalloys in thermal stability [[48](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib48),[49](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib49)]. This durability could lead to longer-lasting, more efficient engines, reducing maintenance costs and emissions, a priority in Industry 4.0’s sustainability focus. Similarly, their corrosion resistance shines in chemical processing or marine environments, where traditional metals degrade, offering a longer lifespan for critical infrastructure [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib12),[50](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib50)]. The adaptability of HEAs further enhances their industrial potential. Unlike traditional alloys with fixed compositions, HEAs can be tailored by adjusting their elemental mix to optimize specific properties, such as lightweight for automotive parts or biocompatibility for medical implants [[37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib37)]. This flexibility aligns with the smart manufacturing ethos of Industry 4.0, where customization and rapid prototyping via techniques like 3D printing are paramount. A CoCrFeNi-based HEA can be optimized for toughness in cryogenic storage systems, aiding advancements in energy and space exploration. This multifunctionality reduces the need for multiple specialized materials, streamlining supply chains and boosting efficiency. Moreover, HEAs’ unique microstructures stabilized by high configurational entropy enable properties that push beyond the limits of earlier industrial materials. Steel powered the First and Second Industrial Revolutions with strength and scalability; semiconductors defined the Third with electronic precision. With their blend of resilience and versatility, HEAs could underpin the Fourth by enabling technologies that integrate physical, digital, and biological systems. Imagine sensors or robotics components that resist wear and extreme conditions while remaining lightweight. HEAs could make this a reality, enhancing the reliability of interconnected, data-driven industrial ecosystems. Their potential isn’t without context. The Fourth Industrial Revolution demands materials that support sustainability, and HEAs' durability could reduce waste by extending product lifecycles. Their use in high-efficiency turbines or battery components in energy sectors could advance renewable technologies [[51](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib51),[52](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589234725000922#bib52)]. Yet, their industrial importance hinges on overcoming challenges like high production costs and scalability. If these hurdles are addressed, perhaps through innovations in manufacturing or computational design, HEAs could rival the revolutionary impact of steel or silicon, cementing their status as a defining material of Industry 4.0. Their superior properties offer a glimpse of a future where industrial limits are redefined, one alloy at a time. ### 1.4. Purpose of the review The purpose of reviewing "The Prospect and Limitation of High Entropy Alloy as 4th Industrial Material" is to critically assess the potential of HEAs to redefine industrial applications while identifying the practical barriers that could impede their widespread adoption. This examination aims to bridge the gap between the theoretical promise of HEAs and their real-world viability, providing a balanced perspective for researchers, engineers, and industry leaders navigating the Fourth Industrial Revolution. By exploring their strengths and shortcomings, the review seeks to inform strategies for leveraging HEAs in cutting-edge technologies and guide future research and development efforts. One primary goal is to evaluate the prospects of HEAs as a transformative material. This involves analyzing how their superior properties align with the demands of Industry 4.0, including smart manufacturing, sustainability, and advanced engineering. The review aims to highlight specific applications where HEAs could outperform traditional materials, such as aerospace components, energy systems, or biomedical devices, and assess their potential to drive innovation in these fields. Doing so establishes a clear case for why HEAs are being considered a candidate for the "4th Industrial Material," building on the legacy of steel, semiconductors, and other historical game-changers. Equally important is the goal of identifying and understanding the limitations of HEAs. This includes examining challenges like complex manufacturing processes, high production costs, and scalability issues that could hinder their transition from laboratory success to industrial staples. The review aims to pinpoint gaps in current knowledge, such as long-term performance under real-world conditions, and assess whether these constraints outweigh the benefits in practical settings. By outlining these obstacles, the analysis provides a reality check against the enthusiasm surrounding HEAs, ensuring expectations remain grounded in feasibility. Another key objective is to foster a forward-looking dialogue. enriched_points.expanded_content: The findings indicate that high-entropy alloys (HEAs) exhibit superior mechanical properties, enhancing material performance in extreme conditions, thus enabling their use in critical applications.
HEAs are increasingly relevant in aerospace, automotive, and biomedical fields, providing innovative solutions for components requiring high strength, corrosion resistance, and thermal stability.

Opportunities exist to explore advanced processing techniques, optimize compositions for specific applications, and investigate long-term performance under real-world conditions to unlock HEAs' full potential.
